questions.text: Who is the highest formal authority in India?
questions.explanation: The President is the head of the state and the highest formal authority.

questions.text: Who actually exercises all governmental powers in India?
questions.explanation: The Prime Minister is the head of the government and exercises real power.

questions.text: The Parliament of India consists of the President and which two houses?
questions.explanation: The Lok Sabha (House of the People) and Rajya Sabha (Council of States) are the two houses.

questions.text: What was the 'Mandal Commission' primarily concerned with?
options: Reservation for Socially and Educationally Backward Classes (SEBC)
questions.explanation: The commission recommended 27% reservation for SEBC in government jobs.

questions.text: Which institution has the power to settle disputes between the government and citizens?
questions.explanation: The judiciary, especially the Supreme Court, acts as an arbiter in disputes involving the government.

questions.text: Who appoints the Prime Minister of India?
questions.explanation: The President appoints the leader of the majority party or coalition in the Lok Sabha as PM.

questions.text: The council of ministers is headed by:
questions.explanation: The Prime Minister leads the Cabinet and other ministers.

questions.text: What is the maximum strength of the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: The maximum strength is 552, though currently elections are held for 543 seats.

questions.text: Rajya Sabha is also known as the ________.
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha is the Upper House and is never fully dissolved, making it permanent.

questions.text: Who presides over the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: The Speaker is elected by the members of the Lok Sabha to conduct its proceedings.

questions.text: Who is the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: The Vice President of India always holds the position of Chairperson of Rajya Sabha.

questions.text: In a dispute over an ordinary law, which house usually prevails because of its larger number?
questions.explanation: In a joint session, the Lok Sabha's view is likely to prevail due to its higher membership.

questions.text: Which house has more power in money matters?
questions.explanation: The Lok Sabha passes the budget and Rajya Sabha can only delay it by 14 days.

questions.text: The 'Political Executive' refers to:
questions.explanation: They are elected by the people for a specific period and take big policy decisions.

questions.text: The 'Permanent Executive' consists of:
questions.explanation: Civil servants stay in office even when the ruling party changes.

questions.text: Why is the Minister more powerful than the civil servant in a democracy?
options: Minister is elected and represents the will of the people
questions.explanation: The Minister is accountable to the people, and in a democracy, the will of the people is supreme.

questions.text: The inner circle of the Council of Ministers, comprising about 20 top-level ministers, is called:
questions.explanation: The Cabinet takes most of the major decisions in the name of the Council of Ministers.

questions.text: If a government loses the 'No-Confidence Motion' in the Lok Sabha:
questions.explanation: Loss of confidence means the government has lost the support of the majority in the house.

questions.text: Who is the Supreme Commander of the defense forces of India?
questions.explanation: This is a key constitutional role of the President.

questions.text: The President of India is elected ________.
options: By an electoral college consisting of MPs and MLAs
questions.explanation: The President is indirectly elected by the representatives of the people.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT a power of the President?
options: Dismissing the PM who has majority support
questions.explanation: The President must appoint the leader with majority support and cannot dismiss them at will.

questions.text: A bill becomes an act only after ________ signs it.
questions.explanation: The President's assent is the final step in the law-making process.

questions.text: 'Judicial Review' means the power of the courts to:
options: Check the constitutional validity of laws and executive actions
questions.explanation: Courts can declare a law invalid if it goes against the Constitution.

questions.text: The 'Integrated Judiciary' in India means:
options: Each state has its own independent law
Decisions of higher courts are binding on lower courts
Courts are under the Prime Minister
questions.explanation: India has a unified structure with the Supreme Court at the top.

questions.text: Once a judge of the Supreme Court is appointed, it is ________ to remove them.
questions.explanation: A judge can only be removed through a difficult impeachment process by two-thirds majority in both houses.

questions.text: PIL stands for:
questions.explanation: PIL allows any citizen to approach the court for a matter of public interest.

questions.text: Who among the following was the PM when the Mandal Commission report was implemented?
questions.explanation: V.P. Singh's government took the decision to implement the report in 1990.

questions.text: An 'Office Memorandum' is:
options: A letter written by a clerk
A formal communication from the government about a decision
questions.explanation: It is a document used to issue orders in the name of the government.

questions.text: The Prime Minister’s power has increased so much that modern democracies are sometimes called ________ form of government.
questions.explanation: The term highlights the central and dominant role of the PM in the cabinet.

questions.text: The Cabinet as a team must ________ all decisions taken by it.
questions.explanation: This is known as collective responsibility.

questions.text: Which institution makes the laws for the whole country?
questions.explanation: Legislating is the primary function of the Parliament.

questions.text: How many members are nominated to the Rajya Sabha by the President?
questions.explanation: These are people who have excelled in fields like literature, science, art, and social service.

questions.text: Who is the head of the state in India?
questions.explanation: The President is the first citizen and represents the nation.

questions.text: A 'Coalition Government' is formed by:
options: One single party with huge majority
Two or more political parties joining together
questions.explanation: Coalitions happen when no single party gets a majority in the house.

questions.text: The power of the PM in a coalition is ________ compared to a single-party government.
questions.explanation: The PM has to accommodate the views and demands of coalition partners.

questions.text: Which of these institutions is responsible for implementing the laws?
questions.explanation: The executive (ministers and civil servants) executes or implements decisions.

questions.text: The term of Lok Sabha is ________ years.
questions.explanation: General elections are held every five years unless the house is dissolved earlier.

questions.text: The term of a Rajya Sabha member is ________ years.
questions.explanation: Members of Rajya Sabha are elected for six years, and one-third of members retire every two years.

questions.text: What does 'Independence of Judiciary' mean?
options: Judiciary is not under the control of legislature or executive
There are no laws for judges
questions.explanation: This allows judges to deliver justice without fear or favor.

questions.text: The Supreme Court of India consists of the ________ and other judges.
questions.explanation: The CJI is the head of the Indian judiciary.
